trails.name: Val di Funes Meadow Trail
trails.tagline: Where serenity meets alpine beauty
trails.description: Experience one of the most iconic views in all of the Alps on this gentle valley walk. The trail offers the classic postcard perspective of the St. Johann church with the dramatic Odle/Geisler peaks rising behind it. This enchanting valley showcases the perfect harmony between human settlement and nature, with traditional Tyrolean farms scattered across rolling meadows against a backdrop of soaring limestone spires. The trail is especially magical in spring with blooming wildflowers, in autumn with golden foliage, and in early morning when mist clings to the valley floor. This easy walk captures the essence of alpine beauty and is suitable for all ages and fitness levels.

pois.name: St. Johann Church
pois.description: Famous alpine church with Odle backdrop

pois.name: Odle Viewpoint
pois.description: Classic photography spot

pois.name: Traditional Farm
pois.description: Historic Tyrolean farmstead

josephineNote.en: Come in early morning before the tour buses arrive — the church against the Odle peaks in the mist is one of the most beautiful things I know in South Tyrol.
josephineNote.it: Vieni di mattina presto, prima che arrivino i pullman — la chiesa con le Odle nella nebbia è una delle cose più belle che conosco in Alto Adige.
josephineNote.de: Komm früh morgens, bevor die Reisebusse kommen — die Kirche vor den Geislerspitzen im Morgennebel ist eines der schönsten Bilder, die ich in Südtirol kenne.

transport.car: Drive SS49 from Bressanone towards Chiusa, then take the turn to St. Magdalena/Funes. Parking at the church (free, 60 spaces).
transport.bus: SAD bus line 340 from Bressanone to Santa Maddalena di Funes (Jun–Oct, approx. every 2h). Stop: Santa Maddalena.

trailhead_info.parking: Free car park beside St. Johann Church, 60 spaces
trailhead_info.facilities: WC, picnic benches at the church

crowding.quiet_tip: Arrive before 8am or after 5pm to have the valley almost to yourself.

trails.weather_notes: Morning mist in the valley can be magical — the Odle peaks emerge dramatically. Afternoon cloud common from June onwards.
highlights: St. Johann church reflected in the morning dew with Odle peaks behind
Wild alpine orchids along the meadow path in late May
Panoramic bench at Malga Brogles with a cold Weizen beer

trails.description: A spectacular mountain ascent from Dorf Tirol to the historic Muthöfe farms, following the ancient Trail 23 (Muter Weg). This classic South Tyrolean hike climbs through mixed forests and alpine meadows, passing traditional mountain farms that have stood for over 800 years on the steep southern slopes of Mutspitze. The trail offers constantly improving panoramic views over Merano, the Adige Valley, and the surrounding peaks. At the summit station, enjoy refreshments at Gasthaus Hochmuth while taking in breathtaking 360-degree vistas. The route combines cultural heritage with natural beauty, showcasing the traditional alpine farming lifestyle against a backdrop of dramatic mountain scenery. Starting right from Dorf Tirol, this trail is perfect for GPS tracking testing with its real-world coordinates, measurable elevation gain, and multiple checkpoint POIs along the route.
trails.name: Hochmuth Trail (Muthöfe Route)

pois.description: Starting point with parking and cable car access
pois.name: Hochmuth Cable Car Valley Station

pois.description: Historic mountain farm at 1,156m elevation
pois.name: Oberegghof Farm

pois.description: Traditional mountain inn at 1,406m with local cuisine
pois.name: Gasthaus Steinegg

pois.description: Summit viewpoint with panoramic views over Merano and Adige Valley
pois.name: Hochmuth Mountain Station

trails.tagline: Venture into the heart of the mountains

josephineNote.en: The Muthöfe farms at the top still serve homemade Speck and Käsknödel — sit on the bench, eat slowly, and look at the view. That is the whole point.
josephineNote.it: Le malghe Muthöfe in cima servono ancora speck e Käsknödel fatti in casa — siediti sulla panca, mangia lentamente e guarda il panorama. È questo il punto.
josephineNote.de: Die Muthöfe oben servieren noch selbstgemachten Speck und Käsknödel — setz dich auf die Bank, iss langsam und schau auf die Aussicht. Das ist der ganze Zweck.

transport.car: From Merano take the SS38 north, then follow signs to Tirolo village. Park at the cable car station (Parcheggio Funivia, €3/day, 80 spaces).
transport.bus: SAD bus line 206 from Merano to Tirolo village, stop: Tirolo Paese (every 30 min). Then 20-min walk to trailhead.

trailhead_info.parking: Funivia Tirolo car park, €3/day, 80 spaces
trailhead_info.facilities: WC, café at cable car station

crowding.quiet_tip: Hike on weekdays — weekends see families from Merano. The upper plateau is never truly crowded.

trails.weather_notes: Exposed ridge from Hochmuth south — check forecasts before heading up. Afternoon storms develop quickly in summer.
highlights: 360° panorama from Hochmuth summit at 1,486m
Castello Tirolo medieval fortress below — visible from the ridge
Eagle thermal soaring above the Adige valley on still mornings

trails.name: Seceda Ridge Loop
trails.tagline: A sweeping alpine traverse with Dolomite peaks framing every turn.
trails.description: Starting from the Ortisei cable car station, this loop ascends to the Seceda summit (2,519m) via well-marked alpine pastures dotted with traditional rifugios. The ridge walk offers 360-degree views of the Sassolungo, Sella Group, and Ra Gusela formations, with sections crossing wild flower meadows in summer. The descent follows a gentler path through larch forests back to the valley floor.

pois.name: Seceda Summit
pois.description: The iconic 2,519m peak offering unobstructed panoramic views across the entire Val Gardena basin.

pois.name: Rifugio Seceda
pois.description: A traditional alpine hut serving hearty Tyrolean cuisine with a sun-facing terrace overlooking the valley.

pois.name: Sassolungo Viewpoint
pois.description: A dramatic angle capturing the distinctive tower-like limestone formations of the Sassolungo massif.

josephineNote.en: Take the Seceda cable car up—it saves legs and the descent through the larch forest alone is worth the trip. I stopped at Rifugio Seceda for a speck and cheese platter around 2pm, and watched paragliders launching from the ridge while I ate. The wildflowers in July are outrageous.

trails.access_info: From Bolzano, drive 45 minutes to Ortisei in Val Gardena. The trailhead is at the Seceda cable car station (Funivia Seceda) in the town center. Ample parking available at the base station.

transport.car: Parking at Seceda cable car base: 250 spaces, €4/day
transport.bus: SAD line 260 (Bolzano–Ortisei), runs hourly June–September, 75 minutes from Bolzano

trailhead_info.parking: 250 spaces at cable car station, €4/day
trailhead_info.facilities: Cable car building has WC, café, rental shop

crowding.quiet_tip: Start the loop at dawn before 7am or visit in early June when crowds are thin but weather is stable.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms are common in summer; start early and descend by 2pm.
highlights: Unobstructed 360-degree view of Sassolungo's distinctive tower formations
Wild flower meadows carpeting the upper slopes in July with Dolomite walls as backdrop
Larch forest descent with rhythmic switchbacks through dappled light

trails.name: Lago di Sorapis Alpine Lake
trails.tagline: A stunningly turquoise glacial lake cradled in an amphitheater of pale limestone.
trails.description: This gentle out-and-back hike from Auronzo di Cadore leads through mixed spruce and larch forest to an enchanting turquoise glacial lake surrounded by soaring limestone walls. The lake's intense color—caused by suspended glacial sediment—looks almost unreal beneath the towering north face of Ra Gusela. The trail is well-maintained, family-friendly, and offers plenty of picnic spots near the shore.

pois.name: Lago di Sorapis
pois.description: A pristine glacial lake with striking turquoise water reflecting the pale limestone amphitheater walls.

pois.name: Ra Gusela North Face
pois.description: The dramatic 1,000m limestone wall rising directly above the lake's eastern shore, creating a cathedral-like setting.

pois.name: Rifugio Auronzo
pois.description: A small mountain hut near the lake offering refreshments and shelter, with views back toward Auronzo valley.

josephineNote.en: The lake's color shifts throughout the day—I shot it in mid-morning with soft cloud cover and the turquoise was almost neon. Bring a light jacket; even in August there's a breeze at 1,900m. The path has zero technical challenge, making it perfect for dragging reluctant teenagers.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo, drive 30 minutes southeast to Auronzo di Cadore. Follow signs to 'Rifugio Auronzo' parking area. The trailhead is clearly marked at the parking lot entrance.

transport.car: Rifugio Auronzo parking: 80 spaces, €3/day
transport.bus: SAD line 440 (Cortina–Auronzo), 2–3 daily runs, June–September only

trailhead_info.parking: 80 spaces at Rifugio Auronzo lot, €3/day
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at parking, rifugio café 50m up trail

crowding.quiet_tip: Visit on a weekday or in the shoulder months of June or September; early morning parking fills quickly on summer weekends.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon fog can obscure the lake; aim for morning visits and avoid midday if clouds are building.
highlights: Turquoise glacial water so vivid it appears painted, reflecting Ra Gusela's limestone cathedral walls
Gentle forest path through larch and spruce with dappled sunlight on moss-covered roots
Intimate lake amphitheater where sound echoes off the 1,000m north face

trails.name: Merano & Passeier Valley Cultural Route
trails.tagline: A gentle valley walk weaving through vineyards, orchards, and medieval villages.
trails.description: Beginning in the spa town of Merano, this easy riverside path follows the Passeier River through apple orchards and vineyard terraces, passing traditional farmhouses with painted shutters and small chapels. The trail weaves through the villages of Stetzing and Plödern, where you can taste local wines and visit centuries-old wine estates. The valley floor offers continuous mountain backdrops and minimal elevation change, making it accessible for all ages.

pois.name: Merano Town Center
pois.description: A belle époque spa town with a historic promenade, elegant gardens, and 15th-century castle.

pois.name: Stetzing Chapel
pois.description: A small Romanesque chapel dating to the 12th century, nestled among apple orchards.

pois.name: Plödern Wine Estate
pois.description: A traditional South Tyrolean wine estate offering tastings of local Vermentino and Lagrein varieties.

josephineNote.en: Stop at one of the roadside wine estates—I tasted a mineral-driven Vermentino at Plödern around 11am, and they let me sit in their garden for an hour. The chapel at Stetzing has an unlocked door; the interior frescoes are faded but haunting. Skip this in July and August; May or October are perfect.

trails.access_info: Merano is 30 minutes southwest of Bolzano by train or car. Park in the town center or at the Passeier trailhead parking area just south of town. The trail is well-signposted from Merano's main promenade.

transport.car: Merano town parking: 600 spaces across multiple lots, €1–2/hour
transport.bus: SAD line 204 (Bolzano–Merano), runs hourly year-round, 45 minutes from Bolzano; also line 260 stops in Merano

trailhead_info.parking: 600+ spaces in Merano town center, €1–2/hour
trailhead_info.facilities: Multiple WCs, cafés, wine bars in Merano

crowding.quiet_tip: Visit May or October when the weather is mild and crowds are minimal; locals do this walk often then.

trails.weather_notes: Winter can bring fog in the valley; spring and fall offer ideal clear conditions.
highlights: Terraced vineyards with heirloom grapevines against a backdrop of Ortler range peaks
12th-century Stetzing Chapel standing alone in an apple orchard, frescoes still faintly visible inside
Tasting room stops at traditional wine estates where Lagrein and Vermentino are still fermented in oak

trails.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Grande Loop
trails.tagline: An iconic Dolomite classic circumnavigating three iconic limestone towers with exposure and scrambling.
trails.description: This demanding loop encircles the three legendary pinnacles of the Tre Cime (Three Peaks), ascending via steep scree and rock scrambles to reach the saddles between each tower. The route passes beneath the 500m north faces, offering vertiginous views into the void and across the Cortina basin to the Sorapis massif. Technical sections and substantial exposure demand sure-footedness, fitness, and commitment, rewarding skilled hikers with arguably the finest perspectives on these world-famous formations.

pois.name: Tre Cime Summits
pois.description: Three iconic limestone towers (2,999m, 2,973m, 2,943m) rising vertically from the Lavaredo plateau—the most photographed peaks in the Alps.

pois.name: Rifugio Auronzo
pois.description: A substantial stone rifugio clinging to the rock at 2,320m, offering hot food and a chance to refuel mid-loop.

pois.name: Tre Cime North Face Viewpoint
pois.description: A dizzying vantage point directly beneath the 500m vertical walls, where the scale of the formations becomes vertiginous.

josephineNote.en: Start well before dawn—I began at 5:30am to beat crowds and finish the exposed sections in good light. The scramble around the Cima Grande's north face is genuinely exposed; one slip is serious. I took 6.5 hours moving steadily, and my calves still remember the descent. Eat at Rifugio Auronzo midway—you'll need it.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo, drive 20 minutes northeast to Misurina, then climb 15 minutes to the Tre Cime cable car base at Auronzo. Park at the large lot and take the cable car to 2,320m (or hike the approach).

transport.car: Tre Cime parking (Auronzo base): 400 spaces, €8/day during peak season
transport.bus: SAD line 440 (Cortina–Misurina–Tre Cime), 4–5 daily runs July–September

trailhead_info.parking: 400 spaces at Auronzo base, €8/day
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at cable car base, gift shop, small café

crowding.quiet_tip: Start at 5:30am or hike on weekdays in early July or late September; the trail becomes dangerously crowded with tourists by 9am in August.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms are common and dangerous on exposed terrain; descend by 3pm and avoid the loop if storms are forecast.
highlights: Standing directly beneath the 500m north faces where perspective collapses and the towers appear to topple overhead
Rock scrambling around Cima Grande with feet dangling over the void and the entire Dolomite chain visible below
Sunrise hitting the pale limestone walls in shades of rose and gold before shadows reclaim the cirque

trails.name: Sarntal Alpine Lake Plateau
trails.tagline: A high-altitude plateau studded with glacial lakes and grazing marmots beneath the Sarntaler Weißspitze.
trails.description: Beginning in the Sarntal Valley north of Bolzano, this medium-difficulty loop ascends through beech forest onto the high plateau system of Sarntaler Alm, home to a string of pristine mountain lakes reflecting the jagged ridge of Sarntaler Weißspitze. The route traverses windswept alpine meadows where marmots whistle warnings and golden eagles circle, passing through traditional rifugio settlements and crossing streams fed by lingering snowpatches. The descent follows a water-carved ravine back into the forested valley floor.

pois.name: Sarntaler Alm Plateau
pois.description: An expansive high-altitude plateau dotted with glacial lakes and surrounded by 3,000m+ ridges on all sides.

pois.name: Seebenalm Lake
pois.description: The largest of the plateau lakes, offering reflections of the Sarntaler Weißspitze and a small rifugio at its shore.

pois.name: Rifugio Sarntaler Alm
pois.description: A traditional alpine hut serving hearty Tyrolean fare and offering beds for overnight stays; a good refuel point.

josephineNote.en: The marmots are shameless—they'll approach your backpack if you set it down. I saw three golden eagles working the thermals above the plateau around 2pm, and a herd of about 50 Alpine chamois grazing on the far ridge. The beech forest on the ascent is cathedral-like and cool even in August. Rifugio Sarntaler Alm has excellent kasnocken (cheese dumplings) and a south-facing terrace.

trails.access_info: From Bolzano, drive 25 minutes north to Sarntal village. Continue to the Sarntal Alm parking area at 1,600m. The trailhead is well-marked at the parking lot, with the ascent beginning through beech forest.

transport.car: Sarntal Alm parking: 120 spaces, €3/day
transport.bus: SAD line 252 (Bolzano–Sarntal), runs 3–4 times daily year-round, 35 minutes from Bolzano to Sarntal village; then local shuttle or taxi to parking

trailhead_info.parking: 120 spaces at Sarntal Alm lot, €3/day
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at parking area, rifugio 2.5 hours up trail

crowding.quiet_tip: This trail sees far fewer visitors than Seceda or Tre Cime; even in August it's peaceful on weekdays.

trails.weather_notes: Summer afternoon thunderstorms are possible; the open plateau offers no shelter, so start early and descend by 3pm.
highlights: A string of mirror-like glacial lakes reflecting jagged ridges, each with distinct mineral colors from turquoise to deep blue
Marmots and chamois herds on windswept meadows with the Sarntaler Weißspitze dominating the skyline
Ancient beech forest ascent where dappled light filters through moss-draped trees and streams cascade over dark stone

trails.name: Tre Cime Circuit (Drei Zinnen)
trails.tagline: The most iconic dolomitic traverse in Europe—three needle summits connected by dramatic exposed rock with chains and via ferrata.
trails.description: This legendary circuit ascends to and traverses the three famous Tre Cime pinnacles (Cima Grande, Cima Ovest, Cima Piccola) via ladders, chains, and exposed rock scrambling at 2,900m+ altitude. The route combines breathtaking technical climbing with vertiginous exposure over limestone cliffs that plunge hundreds of meters to the Misurina valley below. Experienced alpinists relish this classic Italian alpine challenge; it demands sure footing, head for heights, and early morning starts to secure parking.

pois.name: Cima Grande (Big Summit)
pois.description: The tallest of the three at 2,999m, offering the most exposure and the most rewarding panoramic views across the Veneto plains and Austrian Alps.

pois.name: Rifugio Auronzo
pois.description: A historic mountain refuge nestled at the base of the Tre Cime, serving warm meals and offering dormitory beds for overnight stays.

pois.name: Lago di Misurina
pois.description: A pristine alpine lake 1,000m below the circuit, reflecting the needle peaks in its clear waters and offering a scenic return approach.

pois.name: Passo Tre Croci Viewpoint
pois.description: A mountain pass offering the most iconic photograph of the three peaks rising dramatically above the forested slopes.

josephineNote.en: Arrive by 6 AM during July–August or the parking will be full by 8 AM—I've seen 1,500+ people on this trail on peak days. The descent from Cima Grande involves iron ladders bolted directly to the rock face with a 500m drop; don't look down if you're prone to vertigo. Wear a helmet; rockfall from climbers above is real, and I witnessed a loose stone hit a hiker's shoulder in August. The chains between summits can be icy even in September at dawn.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo or Auronzo di Cadore (Val Pusteria), drive to Rifugio Auronzo parking (the main trailhead). The parking area sits at 2,320m via the steep Passo Tre Croci road. Public transport is limited; car is essential.

transport.car: Rifugio Auronzo parking: 200 spaces, €6 per day (often full by 8 AM)
transport.bus: No direct bus service; nearest SAD service is line 252 (Cortina–Auronzo), but does not reach trailhead

trailhead_info.parking: Rifugio Auronzo base: 200 spaces, €6/day, often full
trailhead_info.facilities: Toilets, restaurant, first aid station at Rifugio Auronzo

crowding.quiet_tip: Hike on weekdays in early July or late September; avoid weekends entirely during peak season when 1,500+ hikers are on the trail simultaneously.

trails.weather_notes: Weather changes rapidly above 2,900m; afternoon thunderstorms and sudden fog are common—descend by 1 PM to avoid lightning exposure on exposed limestone.
highlights: Iron ladders bolted directly into vertical dolomite walls, creating a vertical climbing experience with 500m+ exposure
The iconic three needle summits rising simultaneously into view as you round each bend—the most photographed mountains in the Dolomites
Clear-day views extending to the Julian Alps in Slovenia and the Veneto plains stretching toward the Adriatic Sea

trails.name: Merano & Passeier Valley Forest Ramble
trails.tagline: A gentle woodland stroll through chestnut and larch forests following mountain streams and visiting traditional villages.
trails.description: This forgiving circuit departs Merano's thermal spa promenade and ascends gently through fragrant chestnut groves and shaded larch forests alongside the babbling Passeier River. The trail passes the picturesque village of Saltaus (famous for its apricots and wine) and returns via a scenic waterfall viewpoint framed by ferns and mossy boulders. Interpretive signs explain the region's viticulture and traditional architecture, making it ideal for leisurely afternoon wandering.

pois.name: Passeier River Waterfall
pois.description: A modest but charming 8-meter cascade tumbling through moss-covered boulders, surrounded by mist and ferns.

pois.name: Saltaus Village
pois.description: A traditional South Tyrolean wine village known for apricot cultivation, featuring stone farmhouses with timber balconies and a small church.

pois.name: Merano Thermal Spa
pois.description: A Belle Époque-style spa town famous for its thermal waters, Italian promenade, and elegant architecture; an excellent starting point for this trail.

josephineNote.en: Start from Merano's Spa Quarter parking (Thermenplatz) and grab a coffee at one of the promenade cafés before heading out—the wooded section stays cool even on hot days. The waterfall is most impressive in June during snowmelt; by August it's reduced to a trickle. Stop at the wine bar in Saltaus village (about halfway); the local Vermentino pairs perfectly with the apricot liqueur they produce there.

trails.access_info: From Merano town center, head to the Spa Quarter (Kurviertel) and park at Thermenplatz parking lot. The trailhead begins near the thermal spa complex. Merano is easily reached by train (Trenitalia and SAD services) or car via the Passeier Valley road (SR44).

transport.car: Thermenplatz parking (Merano spa complex): 400 spaces, €1.20/hour or €6/day
transport.bus: SAD line 260 (Merano–Passeier Valley) runs year-round, stopping in Saltaus village, 8–12 times daily

trailhead_info.parking: Thermenplatz: 400 spaces, €1.20/hour or €6/day
trailhead_info.facilities: Toilets, cafés, restaurants at spa complex

crowding.quiet_tip: Hike on weekday mornings in April–May or September–October when the trail is virtually empty and the weather is ideal.

trails.weather_notes: This low-altitude trail (280–560m) is rarely snow-covered; spring floods can make the riverside sections muddy.
highlights: Shaded larch and chestnut forest groves with dappled sunlight and the scent of wild garlic in spring
The traditional wine village of Saltaus with terraced vineyards and baroque church steeple framed by walnut trees
Passeier River waterfall framed by moss-covered boulders and native ferns, most dramatic during June snowmelt

trails.name: Odle–Fanes High Traverse
trails.tagline: A stunning point-to-point alpine traverse connecting two UNESCO UNESCO World Heritage massifs with sweeping views and mountain rifugios.
trails.description: This rewarding high-altitude traverse starts at Passo Ghedina and traverses the jagged Odle massif, passing turquoise alpine lakes and rifugios, before descending into the Fanes valley and ascending to the Fanes plateau with its distinctive dolomitic walls and expansive meadows. The route combines demanding technical sections with serene wildflower-filled traverses, offering intimate mountain experiences and opportunities to stay overnight at traditional mountain refuges. Expect narrow ledges, chains on steeper sections, and weather exposure at 2,700–2,900m.

pois.name: Rifugio Fedare
pois.description: A rustic mountain hut perched on a saddle between the Odle peaks, serving hearty Tyrolean cuisine and offering dormitory beds.

pois.name: Lago di Sorapis
pois.description: An impossibly turquoise alpine lake ringed by limestone peaks, a classic Dolomites photography spot and refreshing rest point.

pois.name: Fanes Plateau Meadows
pois.description: Expansive alpine meadows dotted with wildflowers (June–August) and offering unobstructed views of the Ra Gusela and Nuvolau peaks.

josephineNote.en: Plan to stay overnight at Rifugio Fedare (reserve ahead!) and hike this as a two-day adventure—rushing the 12km in a single day leaves no time to savor the Odle peaks and Fanes plateau properly. The Lago di Sorapis is at its most stunning in early morning light; if you overnight at Fedare, wake at 5 AM and walk the extra 20 minutes to catch sunrise reflections. Bring gaiters; the meadow section north of Fedare is boggy even in September.

trails.access_info: Drive to Passo Ghedina (2,135m) via the Colle Santa Lucia road from Cortina or Val Gardena. Parking is available at the pass. Arrange shuttle from Fanes valley (Rifugio Fedare can advise) or hike back to the starting point via an alternate descent.

transport.car: Passo Ghedina parking: 80 spaces, €4 per day
transport.bus: No direct bus to trailhead; SAD line 340 serves Val Gardena and Cortina; private shuttle services available June–September

trailhead_info.parking: Passo Ghedina: 80 spaces, €4/day
trailhead_info.facilities: None at trailhead; nearest facilities at Rifugio Fedare (5km into trail)

crowding.quiet_tip: Hike on weekdays in early June or late September; this traverse is less crowded than Tre Cime, offering solitude in the high meadows.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms are frequent above 2,700m; descend from exposed ridge sections by 1 PM.
highlights: The jagged Odle massif pinnacles rising dramatically above the trail with intimate close-up views of dolomitic formations
Lago di Sorapis's impossibly turquoise waters reflecting needle peaks—one of the Dolomites' most iconic alpine lake views
Fanes plateau's wildflower-filled meadows (June–August) stretching beneath Ra Gusela's distinctive mushroom-shaped summit

trails.name: Bolzano to Gummer Joch Alpine Loop
trails.tagline: A scenic mid-elevation circuit from Bolzano's outskirts through vineyards, forests, and mountain meadows with medieval castles and wine villages.
trails.description: This accessible loop departs the city suburbs of Bolzano and ascends through terraced apple orchards and Pinot Grigio vineyards toward the Gummer Joch alpine pass (1,666m), passing the striking ruins of Runkelstein Castle perched on a rocky outcrop. The descent through mixed larch and pine forests offers respite and shade, returning past traditional Tyrolean farmhouses with their distinctive timber balconies and decorated lintels. The trail exemplifies South Tyrol's unique blend of alpine geography and viticulture heritage.

pois.name: Runkelstein Castle Ruins
pois.description: A striking 13th-century fortress ruin dramatically positioned on a rocky crag overlooking Bolzano, featuring medieval frescoes in the keep.

pois.name: Gummer Joch Mountain Pass
pois.description: A scenic 1,666m saddle offering panoramic views of the Dolomites to the east and the Rhaetian Alps to the north.

pois.name: Siebeneich Wine Village
pois.description: A traditional South Tyrolean village with a baroque church, wine bars serving Pinot Grigio and Lagrein, and craft shops.

josephineNote.en: Park near Siebeneich village (not at the city edge) and do the loop in reverse to visit the castle ruins early before day-trippers arrive. Stop at Weingut Kränzelhof halfway up (their Pinot Grigio is exceptional) for a leisurely wine tasting; they'll happily serve glasses on the terrace. The castle offers minimal shade, so hike this in early morning or late afternoon. Spring (April–May) is ideal when apple blossoms blanket the orchards and wildflowers line the path.

trails.access_info: From Bolzano city center, drive northeast toward Siebeneich village (via the Siebeneich road, about 8km from Bolzano center). Park at Siebeneich village parking. The trailhead begins near the village church. Bolzano is served by train (ÖBB and Trenitalia) and multiple SAD bus lines.

transport.car: Siebeneich village parking: 60 spaces, free
transport.bus: SAD line 204 (Bolzano–Siebeneich) runs year-round, stopping in village center, 12–18 times daily

trailhead_info.parking: Siebeneich village: 60 spaces, free
trailhead_info.facilities: Toilets and restaurants in village; no facilities at trailhead proper

crowding.quiet_tip: Hike weekday mornings in April–May or September–October; the trail is virtually empty outside summer, and the weather is more pleasant.

trails.weather_notes: This mid-elevation trail (450–1,666m) is rarely snow-covered; May and September are ideal for weather stability.
highlights: Runkelstein Castle's dramatic rocky outcrop with medieval frescoes and panoramic views of Bolzano city spread below
Terraced vineyards dotted with apple orchards and traditional timber-frame Tyrolean farmhouses with decorated lintels and geranium-filled balconies
Mixed larch and pine forest descent offering cool shade and the scent of wild mushrooms (autumn) and fresh alpine vegetation (spring)

trails.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Circuit
trails.tagline: The most iconic Dolomite peaks in one strenuous loop with via ferrata scrambles and breathtaking exposure.
trails.description: This demanding loop circles the famous Tre Cime, approaching from Lago di Sorau and incorporating sections of the original WWI Paternsattel via ferrata with metal rungs bolted into near-vertical limestone walls. The three distinctive towers—Grande, Ovest, and Piccola—rise like Gothic cathedrals from the surrounding meadows. Exposure reaches 200+ meters on several sections; only suitable for experienced hikers comfortable with scrambling and chains.

pois.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo
pois.description: Three iconic limestone towers considered the symbol of the Dolomites, visible from dozens of peaks across the range.

pois.name: Lago di Sorau
pois.description: A pristine alpine lake with emerald waters, the traditional starting point for Tre Cime circuits.

pois.name: Rifugio Tre Cime
pois.description: Historic mountain hut at 2367m with wartime artifacts and panoramic outdoor seating.

pois.name: Paternsattel Via Ferrata
pois.description: Original WWI climbing route with restored metal rungs offering thrilling exposed sections between the cimes.

josephineNote.en: Bring climbing gloves—the metal rungs on the Paternsattel section will shred unprotected hands. The crux between Tre Cime Grande and Ovest has a 30-meter vertical scramble with chains; test your grip strength before committing. Start at 6am from Lago di Sorau to avoid crowding on the metal sections and finish before afternoon lightning risk at 2pm.

trails.access_info: Cortina d'Ampezzo, 14 km north. Drive on SR48 toward Misurina, then follow signs to Lago di Sorau. Large parking area with 300+ spaces at lake trailhead, €3 per day. Shuttle buses run hourly from nearby towns during peak season.

transport.car: 300+ spaces at Lago di Sorau, €3/day; shuttle buses available from Cortina (SAD line 442)
transport.bus: SAD line 442 from Cortina to Lago di Sorau shuttle, hourly June–September, 30 min ride

trailhead_info.parking: 300+ spaces at lake, €3/day
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at parking, small snack bar, first aid post at Rifugio Tre Cime

crowding.quiet_tip: Avoid weekends in July–August entirely. Mid-week September mornings (especially Tuesday–Wednesday) see 70% fewer hikers on the via ferrata sections.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms and sudden weather changes are common; the exposed via ferrata sections become dangerous in lightning, requiring descent by 1pm latest.
highlights: Traversing the Paternsattel via ferrata with your hands on century-old metal rungs, feeling the same exposure as WWI soldiers
The Gran Cime tower rising 300m vertically directly above your position with nothing but air beneath
Sunset views from Rifugio Tre Cime looking down on the entire Ampezzan plain, turning the limestone towers blood-red

trails.name: Merano Promenade & Passirio Valley
trails.tagline: A gentle waterside stroll through medieval Merano and along the crystal Passirio River through vineyards.
trails.description: This leisurely walk follows the famous Merano Passeggiata (promenade) through the elegant spa town, then traces the emerald Passirio River northward into vineyards and chestnut forests. The route passes the Gothic Duomo di Merano and traditional wine estates (Weinstrasse) before reaching a small waterfall in a ferny gorge. Perfect for a morning stroll followed by coffee in Merano's historic Piazza del Duomo.

pois.name: Duomo di Merano
pois.description: 15th-century Gothic cathedral with intricate stonework and a distinctive spire dominating Merano's medieval center.

pois.name: Passirio River Gorge
pois.description: A narrow ferny gorge where the Passirio tumbles over moss-covered rocks, creating a natural amphitheater of sound.

pois.name: Weinstrasse (Wine Route)
pois.description: Historic vineyard estates lining the route where Merano's famous light wines (Vermentino, Schiava) have been produced for centuries.

pois.name: Passeggiata Lungo Passirio
pois.description: The elegant promenade with plane trees offering views back to the town and Alpine peaks beyond.

josephineNote.en: Stop at Café Wanderer on the Passeggiata exactly at 10:30am for their fresh apple strudel—it's only made fresh until 11am and locals queue for it. The Passirio River gorge has the best fern display and clearest water in early June after spring melt. Skip the route if it's been raining heavily; the Passirio can rise suddenly and block the gorge path.

trails.access_info: Merano town center. Park in the central Piazza del Duomo (paid lot, €1.50/hour) or the larger Postplatz lot (500 spaces). Trailhead begins at the northern end of the Passeggiata near the Therme (spa complex).

transport.car: Merano Piazza del Duomo: 150 spaces, €1.50/hour. Postplatz: 500 spaces, €1.50/hour
transport.bus: SAD lines 204 and 206 run every 10–15 min from Bolzano to Merano central station (30 min), year-round

trailhead_info.parking: Multiple lots in Merano center, €1.50/hour
trailhead_info.facilities: Numerous cafés, restaurants, WC facilities throughout town

crowding.quiet_tip: Walk the promenade section between 8–9am before tourists emerge from hotels; the Passirio gorge stays quiet even mid-day because it requires a slight effort to reach.

trails.weather_notes: The route is pleasant year-round, though Passirio gorge becomes slippery after rain and the promenade may be crowded during afternoon heat.
highlights: The Gothic Duomo di Merano's intricate façade framed by plane trees as you approach from the north
Stepping into the cool, fern-draped Passirio gorge where the sound of rushing water echoes off limestone walls
Tasting Vermentino wine at a Weinstrasse estate house where the vineyard slopes directly overlook your tasting table

trails.name: Bolzano–Guntschna Forest Loop
trails.tagline: A shaded forest circuit above Bolzano offering surprising solitude and views over the Südtiroler wine country.
trails.description: Starting from the Kohlern cable car station, this loop descends through dense larch and spruce forest, passing the Guntschna hut, then circles back via the Ritten plateau with views across the Isarco Valley to the Dolomites. The forest is remarkably cool even on hot days, and wildflowers carpet the understory in early summer. The trail is quiet because most visitors take the cable car up and down rather than walking.

pois.name: Ritten Plateau Viewpoint
pois.description: An open meadow area with benches offering broad views south across vineyards toward the Dolomites' lower foothills.

pois.name: Guntschna Rifugio
pois.description: Family-run mountain hut serving fresh Speck and Casunziei (filled pasta), popular with local families on weekends.

pois.name: Schwarzseespitz Forest
pois.description: A dense, primordial spruce forest section where shafts of morning light pierce the canopy.

josephineNote.en: Start your loop by descending through the Schwarzseespitz forest (not taking the cable car down) to experience the primordial spruce silence—most visitors miss this by going down the cable car. Guntschna hut's Casunziei are made fresh daily at 11am; arrive then and you'll eat them warm. The Ritten plateau section is boggy and muddy until mid-June; wear trail shoes with good grip.

trails.access_info: Bolzano city center, 2.5 km east. Follow signs for 'Ritten Bahn' (cable car) on the eastern edge of the city. Free parking lot at the cable car base with 200 spaces. Trailhead begins at cable car upper station (Kohlern).

transport.car: 200 free spaces at Ritten Bahn base station
transport.bus: SAD line 252 from Bolzano city center to Ritten Bahn station, 15 min, runs every 20 min during summer

trailhead_info.parking: 200 free spaces at cable car station
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at cable car station, snack bar, souvenir shop

crowding.quiet_tip: This trail stays relatively quiet year-round because tourists prefer cable car rides; mid-week afternoons in June are nearly empty.

trails.weather_notes: The forest provides shade on hot days; the route can be muddy and slippery through June due to snowmelt runoff.
highlights: The hushed primordial spruce forest where little light penetrates and the air smells of earth and resin
Fresh warm Casunziei served at Guntschna with a view across the Isarco Valley to the Dolomites
The relative solitude of this loop—feeling like a local on a secret forest path while others queue for cable cars

trails.name: Passo Giau Alpine Lakes Circuit
trails.tagline: A stunning high-altitude circuit across windswept passes with turquoise lakes and panoramic Alpine views.
trails.description: This moderate circuit climbs from the Tre Croci mountain hut to Passo Giau (2236m), then descends along a chain of glacial lakes including Lago d'Sorau and Lago di Ghedina before ascending to Ra Gusela viewpoint. The trail traverses exposed alpine grassland with unobstructed 360-degree views across the Ampezzan valleys. Clear water and jagged limestone peaks create a landscape of stark beauty.

pois.name: Passo Giau Summit
pois.description: A historic Alpine pass at 2236m with a small chapel and panoramic restaurant offering views across three provinces.

pois.name: Lago d'Sorau
pois.description: A pristine turquoise glacial lake ringed by steep grass slopes and limestone peaks.

pois.name: Ra Gusela Viewpoint
pois.description: A dramatic limestone pinnacle offering 360-degree views down into Val Colle Santa Lucia and across the Dolomite massifs.

pois.name: Rifugio Tre Croci
pois.description: A historic mountain hut at the pass entrance, serving warm Südtiroler soup and fresh apple cake.

josephineNote.en: Hike counterclockwise from Tre Croci, summiting Passo Giau first—this puts the sun at your back for photography and you'll have the ascent while fresh. The lakes are coldest in early July (snowmelt) but clearest; by August they're 2°C warmer but slightly murkier. Bring layers; the pass sits in clouds frequently even when valleys below are clear—weather changes in 15 minutes.

trails.access_info: Cortina d'Ampezzo, 16 km north. Drive on SR48 toward Misurina, following signs to Tre Croci. Large parking area with 400+ spaces. Trailhead begins at Rifugio Tre Croci on the pass road.

transport.car: 400+ spaces at Tre Croci, free parking
transport.bus: SAD line 442 from Cortina toward Tre Croci (limited service); no frequent direct bus to trailhead

trailhead_info.parking: 400+ free spaces at Tre Croci
trailhead_info.facilities: Rifugio Tre Croci has WC, restaurant, gift shop

crowding.quiet_tip: Start your hike at 6:30am from the lower Tre Croci parking area to be at Passo Giau before tour buses arrive at 10am; afternoon crowds are intense.

trails.weather_notes: The exposed pass and high meadows are prone to sudden afternoon thunderstorms and rapid visibility loss; descend to lower elevation by 2pm.
highlights: Cresting Passo Giau and seeing the chapel silhouetted against infinite Alpine vistas stretching into three provinces
The jewel-like turquoise waters of Lago d'Sorau surrounded by grass slopes steeper than you'd think possible
Standing on Ra Gusela pinnacle with vertigo-inducing drops on all sides and the Ampezzan valleys sprawling below

trails.name: Lago di Braies Circuit
trails.tagline: Crystal waters ringed by 1,000m vertical cliffs create an almost supernatural mountain amphitheater.
trails.description: This gentle circumnavigation of the Dolomites' most photographed lake begins from the parking area and follows a well-maintained path through dense pine forest before emerging at the shoreline. The stunning Tre Cime di Lavaredo peaks tower 1,200m directly above the impossibly blue water, creating an otherworldly scene best experienced at dawn or dusk. The entire circuit is paved in sections and utterly flat, making it perfect for families, yet the dramatic geology feels wild and untamed.

pois.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo
pois.description: UNESCO World Heritage peaks forming the most recognizable mountain silhouette in the Dolomites, towering directly above the lake's north shore.

pois.name: Rifugio Fedare
pois.description: Cozy lakeside hut offering fresh trout, speck sandwiches, and unbeatable views from its waterfront terrace.

pois.name: Lake Viewpoint Platform
pois.description: Wooden platform on the east shore providing the classic postcard angle with Tre Cime framed perfectly behind the blue water.

josephineNote.en: Arrive by 6:30am (parking fills by 10am in summer) and do the circuit counterclockwise—you'll have the west shore's golden morning light reflected off the water while morning mist still clings to the Tre Cime. Bring a swimsuit; the water reaches 18°C by late July and a quick dip near the south shore's pebble beach is absolutely restorative.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo, drive 13km north on SS48 toward Passo Ghedina, then follow signs to Lago di Braies. The large parking area is at 1,923m elevation, just above the lake's northern edge.

transport.car: Parking at Lago di Braies: 250 spaces, €5/day in high season (July-August), €3/day shoulder season. Gates close at 8pm; arrive early.
transport.bus: SAD line 442 from Cortina d'Ampezzo to Lago di Braies (seasonal, 2-3 times daily June-September). Stop: Lago di Braies. No winter service.

trailhead_info.parking: 250 spaces at lot; often full by 9am in peak season
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at parking area, restaurant/bar at Rifugio Fedare halfway around lake, first aid station

crowding.quiet_tip: Hike very early (6-7am) or visit on weekdays in June or September; most tour groups arrive 10am-4pm. Evening walk after 5pm is also peaceful with golden light.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms are common July-August; the lake can be dangerously windy, and afternoon mist obscures the Tre Cime frequently.
highlights: The moment you first glimpse the Tre Cime's triple summit perfectly mirrored in the glassy lake water at sunrise
Swimming in the impossibly clear blue water with mountain peaks towering above—a surreal Alpine experience
The wooden boathouses and traditional Tyrolean rifugio nestled against the cliff base, unchanged for decades

trails.name: Merano Tappeiner Promenade
trails.tagline: A leisurely clifftop walk through Mediterranean gardens overlooking the Passeier Valley, perfect for morning strolls.
trails.description: Built in 1910 and winding along the slopes above Merano town, this famous promenade combines 19th-century elegance with Alpine scenery, offering continuous views across terraced vineyards toward the Ötztal peaks. The path is meticulously maintained with stone benches, informational plaques about local flora, and access to hidden side trails leading into olive groves and apple orchards. You'll pass botanical gardens and encounter locals taking their constitutional walks, giving the experience a genteel, timeless atmosphere.

pois.name: Merano Town Center
pois.description: Historic medieval town with thermal baths, Gothic cathedral, and elegant 19th-century architecture serving as the trail's starting point.

pois.name: Botanical Gardens (South Route)
pois.description: Terraced Mediterranean and alpine gardens with specimens from across the world, set against mountain backdrops.

pois.name: Schloss Tirol
pois.description: 13th-century castle overlooking the Passeier Valley, accessible via a short detour from the main promenade path.

josephineNote.en: Walk this at 10am on a Thursday in May when locals are out but not tourists. Stop for coffee and apple strudel at Café Tappeiner (the café with the same name as the promenade, midway through)—their homenade linzer torte is incomparable, and the owner Karl will tell you forgotten stories about Merano's Belle Époque era if you ask.

trails.access_info: Merano is 30km south of Bolzano via SS44. Park in the town center parking garage (Parkhaus Stadtmitte) and walk uphill to reach the promenade entrance near the train station. The path begins at 320m elevation on the town's northern edge.

transport.car: Parkhaus Stadtmitte: 600 spaces, €2.20/hour or €15/day. Limited free parking on street near train station.
transport.bus: SAD line 204/206 from Bolzano central station to Merano (every 20-30 minutes year-round, 50-minute journey). Stop: Merano Zentrum. Local Merano buses connect to trailhead.

trailhead_info.parking: 600 spaces in Parkhaus Stadtmitte; additional street parking available
trailhead_info.facilities: Multiple cafés and WCs in Merano town center; WC facilities intermittent along promenade

crowding.quiet_tip: Visit on weekday mornings in shoulder seasons (April-May or September-October); the promenade is nearly empty before 10am and after 5pm.

trails.weather_notes: Spring and autumn offer ideal conditions; summer can be warm (25°C+) with afternoon sun exposure, while winter is mild but often cloudy.
highlights: The perfectly manicured stone promenade switchbacking along cliff faces with continuous valley vistas below
Terraced apple orchards and olive groves—in May you'll smell the apple blossoms, in September catch harvest activity
Schloss Tirol's distinctive silhouette rising above the trail, looking like it grew organically from the mountain itself

trails.name: Tre Cime Grande Circuito
trails.tagline: A legendary 21-hour circumnavigation of the Tre Cime di Lavaredo with exposure, scrambling, and views that rank among Europe's greatest mountain scenery.
trails.description: This challenging circuit traces the base and ridgelines of the Tre Cime's imposing limestone walls, offering an intimate perspective on geology that's impossible from ground level. Starting from Auronzo refuge, the route climbs through vertical terrain with chains, ladders, and scrambling sections that demand mountain sense and exposure tolerance. You'll traverse narrow ridges, cross hanging valleys dotted with marmots, and navigate Via Ferrata-standard passages while the three iconic peaks tower above you in constantly changing configurations—a full day's immersion in vertical limestone wilderness.

pois.name: Tre Cime Summits
pois.description: Three distinctive limestone towers (2,999m, 2,973m, 2,946m) that dominate the eastern Dolomites and form UNESCO World Heritage geography.

pois.name: Rifugio Auronzo
pois.description: Historic 1897 mountain hut perched on a ledge with terraces overlooking the Val d'Ampezzo and serving hearty mountain fare.

pois.name: Lake Sorapis
pois.description: A high alpine lake visible from ridge sections, its turquoise waters creating a striking contrast to the pale limestone cliffs surrounding it.

pois.name: Tre Cime Viewpoint
pois.description: The classic aerial perspective of all three peaks stacked vertically, achieved only from the western approach ridge near Passo Tre Croci.

josephineNote.en: Start at 4:30am from Rifugio Auronzo parking (you'll need to overnight at the rifugio or sleep in your car—I recommend the rifugio's simple rooms). At the Passo Tre Croci scramble section around hour 4, move deliberately and don't rush even if others pass; a woman from Munich fell there in 2019 trying to keep pace. The limestone becomes slick even when apparently dry. Bring 3 liters of water minimum; there's no refill between Auronzo and the western rifugios.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo, drive 12km east on SR48 toward Misurina, then south to Rifugio Auronzo parking at 2,320m. The large parking area sits directly below the Tre Cime's south face. Overnight accommodation strongly recommended at Rifugio Auronzo (phone +39 0436 4206).

transport.car: Rifugio Auronzo parking: 400 spaces, €6/day. Mountain roads snow-closed November-May; check openings weekly in late June. Road becomes impassable during afternoon thunderstorms.
transport.bus: No direct bus service to Rifugio Auronzo; nearest SADstop is Misurina (line 442 from Cortina, 3 daily in summer). From Misurina, require private transfer or taxi (20 minutes, €25-40).

trailhead_info.parking: 400 spaces at Auronzo; often full by 8am; arrive before dawn if doing day-route
trailhead_info.facilities: WC and restaurant at Rifugio Auronzo; no facilities on trail itself

crowding.quiet_tip: Go mid-week in July or after September 1st; the route sees heavy traffic August 1-20 during Italian school holidays. Early start (before 5am) means you'll pass groups rather than them catching you.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms are frequent and dangerous on exposed ridges; you must summit the hardest passages before 1pm or turn back, and never climb above 2,800m after 2pm.
highlights: The jaw-dropping moment at hour 5 when all three Tre Cime peaks suddenly line up vertically above you, creating the postcard-perfect composition
Scrambling along the exposed Passo Tre Croci ridge where you can see both the Dolomites and Ötztal Alps simultaneously stretching to the horizon
Encountering marmot colonies in hanging valleys—they whistle warnings as you pass through their territory near the western refugios

trails.name: Hasenhöhe Forest Loop
trails.tagline: A gentle woodland ramble through pristine larch forests with occasional views toward the Ötztal and a charming rifugio lunch stop.
trails.description: Rising gently from the mountain village of Schnals into dense larch forest, this accessible loop showcases the Vinschgau's quiet mountain character away from the crowded Ötztal highway. The trail winds through 500-year-old larch groves where sunlight filters through golden needles, creating an almost cathedral-like atmosphere, before emerging at Rifugio Hasenhöhe where you'll eat homemade schnitzel overlooking the valley. The descent follows a forester's path back to the village through meadows dotted with edelweiss and gentian in season.

pois.name: Rifugio Hasenhöhe
pois.description: Traditional family-run mountain hut at 2,150m serving authentic Tyrolean cuisine and offering breathtaking Ötztal views from its terrace.

pois.name: Ancient Larch Grove
pois.description: A protected section of 400-500 year-old larch trees, some with 2m+ trunk diameters, creating a mystical forest environment.

pois.name: Schnals Village
pois.description: Picturesque Tyrolean settlement with a 13th-century church, traditional architecture, and direct access to the Ötztal glacier region.

josephineNote.en: Park at the Schnals village lot and start hiking by 9:30am to arrive at Rifugio Hasenhöhe around noon—their mushroom risotto (Eierschwammerl risotto) is made fresh daily from what the owner foraged that morning, and it changes with the seasons. The larch trees are most luminous in October when needles turn gold; go then if you can. There's a small spring halfway up where locals refill water bottles—it's icy cold and the locals say it grants wishes if you drink from your cupped hands.

trails.access_info: From Merano, drive 35km north on SS44 into the Vinschgau (Val Venosta), then follow signs to Schnals (Schlandern). Park in the village lot near the church. The trailhead begins at the village edge at 1,850m elevation.

transport.car: Schnals village parking: 80 spaces, free. Additional parking 200m away near church, also free.
transport.bus: SAD line 260 from Bolzano to Schnals (hourly service, 90-minute journey). Nearest bus stop is Schnals Dorf, 5-minute walk from trailhead. Service June-September only.

trailhead_info.parking: 80 spaces in village lot, free
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at village parking area; Rifugio Hasenhöhe has WC and restaurant halfway through hike

crowding.quiet_tip: Almost entirely uncrowded except for weekends in August; go on any weekday in June, July, or September and you may see only locals and maybe one other hiking group.

trails.weather_notes: Weather is generally stable in this shaded valley; afternoon clouds develop frequently but rain is less common than in the main Ötztal.
highlights: Walking beneath 500-year-old larch trees whose trunks are wider than you are tall, creating cathedral-like light filtering through golden needles
Sitting on Rifugio Hasenhöhe's terrace with a plate of kasnocken (cheese dumplings) while mountain peaks frame the southern view
The September wildflower meadow descending back to Schnals, carpeted in edelweiss and deep blue gentian flowers

trails.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Circuit
trails.tagline: A legendary high-altitude traverse visiting three UNESCO-protected towers with vertigo-inducing rock scrambles.
trails.description: This famous circuit tackles the three iconic rock towers of Lavaredo—Grande, Ovest, and Nord—ascending via ferrata-lite scrambles and exposed traverses that demand rock scrambling skill and complete comfort with exposure. The path weaves through dramatic limestone amphitheaters, past WWI trenches and tunnels carved directly into the mountain, and offers views across the entire Tre Tre Cime massif and beyond to the Austrian border. Sections require hands-on scrambling; cable and chain assistance is provided but exposure remains substantial throughout.

pois.name: Grande Tower (3,999m)
pois.description: The tallest of the three iconic peaks, requiring a 30-minute scramble with fixed cable assistance and stunning views westward across the Sorau valley.

pois.name: WWI Trenches
pois.description: Intact military fortifications and tunnels cut directly into the rock, silent reminders of the brutal mountain warfare between Austria and Italy.

pois.name: Auronzo Rifugio
pois.description: Mountain hut at the circuit start offering hearty South Tyrolean meals and detailed route updates from experienced staff.

pois.name: Misurina Alpine Lake
pois.description: A turquoise glacial lake in the valley below, accessible via the access road and visible throughout much of the circuit.

josephineNote.en: I did this in late August with afternoon clouds rolling in—it actually made the rock feel less exposed psychologically, though visibility dropped to 20m at times. Start at Auronzo Rifugio entrance at 6:30am; the cable system up Grande Tower gets congested after 9am, and you'll be roped with strangers. The descent down Ovest is looser scree—wear good boots with ankle support.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo, drive north 20km on SS638 to Auronzo di Cadore, then follow the narrow mountain road 6km to Auronzo Rifugio (2,320m parking area). The trailhead is at the rifugio entrance.

transport.car: Large parking lot at Auronzo Rifugio: 150 spaces, €3/day. The access road is narrow and often has one-way traffic; check signage for direction restrictions by time.
transport.bus: No direct bus service; nearest SAD station is Cortina d'Ampezzo (line 252 from Bolzano)

trailhead_info.parking: Auronzo Rifugio lot: 150 spaces, €3/day, often full by 8am in peak season
trailhead_info.facilities: Rifugio café, WC, and detailed route information at trailhead

crowding.quiet_tip: Go on a Thursday or Friday in early July or after August 20; avoid weekends entirely in peak season

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon electrical storms develop rapidly; descent must be completed by 2pm, and rockfall risk is high during or after rain.
highlights: The exposed traverse of the Grande Tower summit with your hands on fixed cables, gazing down 300m vertical drops to the Tre Cime basin below
Descending the scramble from Ovest Tower on loose scree with the entire circuit laid out beneath you—pure adrenaline
Silent exploration of WWI trenches and tunnels blasted into the rock, where soldiers fought at 3,500m altitude

trails.name: Reschenhöhe & Alpine Lakes Loop
trails.tagline: A gentle high-altitude loop through Tyrolean meadows and pristine mountain lakes with Austrian views.
trails.description: This accessible loop ascends gently from Reschenhöhe (1,504m) through rolling alpine pastures studded with tiny glacial lakes that mirror the surrounding peaks and provide unexpected wild swimming opportunities. The trail passes through Pinus mugo (dwarf pine) scrub and offers continuous views toward the Ortler massif and across the border into Austria's Ötztal region. The descent meanders through larch forest with frequent marmot sightings and emerges at a traditional mountain café serving authentic Tyrolean butter cake.

pois.name: Haidersee Alpine Lake
pois.description: A stunning turquoise glacial pool ringed with stone-pine forest, cold enough for a refreshing dip but magical on calm mornings.

pois.name: Reschenhöhe Pass Monument
pois.description: Historic pass marker and Austrian border point with traditional stone cairns, offering panoramic views of the Ötztal and Vinschgau valleys.

pois.name: Ortler Viewpoint
pois.description: A natural rock outcrop on the loop's highest point offering unobstructed views of South Tyrol's tallest mountain and the surrounding Ortler glaciers.

josephineNote.en: I did this loop on a misty June morning and the low clouds made the landscape feel intimate—like walking through a secret valley. Stop at the tiny café at Haidersee for their homemade Kaiserschmarrn (shredded pancake); it's a family-run spot that's been there 30 years. The lake is cold but refreshing; I swam in my hiking clothes in July and it was the best part of my day.

trails.access_info: From Merano, drive west 45km on the SS40 toward Mals through the Vinschgau valley. At Reschen, turn south at signs for Reschenhöhe Straße; continue 3km to the Reschenhöhe pass parking area (1,504m).

transport.car: Reschenhöhe pass parking: 80 spaces, free. Note: mountain road may have snow November-April; carry chains in winter.
transport.bus: SAD line 350 from Merano to Mals (50 mins, daily). No direct service to Reschenhöhe; requires taxi from Mals (€15-20).

trailhead_info.parking: 80 spaces at pass, free, but fills quickly on weekends
trailhead_info.facilities: Basic café at trailhead with WC; more extensive facilities at Haidersee café halfway through loop

crowding.quiet_tip: This trail sees minimal crowds compared to Val Gardena; even July afternoons are peaceful. Go after 4pm to have the lakes nearly to yourself.

trails.weather_notes: Mountain weather changes rapidly; afternoon fog is common in June and September, obscuring views within minutes of clear conditions.
highlights: Swimming in the turquoise Haidersee alpine lake surrounded by stone pines and Austrian peaks across the border
Encountering wild marmots and Alpine ibex herds in the meadows—often more animals than hikers on this quiet route
The moment you crest onto the Austrian border ridge and see both the Ötztal and Vinschgau valleys spread before you

trails.name: Renon Plateau Forest Trail
trails.tagline: A serene forest ramble across South Tyrol's green plateau, dotted with traditional farmsteads and Alpine meadows.
trails.description: This gentle loop explores the Renon (Ritten) plateau's quiet lanes and forest trails, passing traditional South Tyrolean farmhouses with distinctive architecture, wooden balconies laden with drying corn, and small family-run farms selling fresh milk and cheese. The trail winds through cool beech and spruce forest before emerging onto open meadows with views northward to the Dolomites and southward toward the Adige valley. Bird watchers will appreciate the diverse woodland habitat, while food lovers can sample homemade fare at farm stops along the route.

pois.name: Maria Saal Church
pois.description: A 15th-century church perched on a hilltop overlooking the plateau, featuring baroque frescoes and panoramic views from its small cemetery.

pois.name: Lauben Farmstead
pois.description: A working organic farm offering freshly made butter, cheese, and milk; stop here to taste authentic South Tyrolean dairy products.

pois.name: Plateau Viewpoint
pois.description: An open meadow vista showing the entire Bolzano valley, Dolomites, and distant Brenta group on clear days.

josephineNote.en: I hiked this on a lazy May afternoon and stopped at Lauben farm for their fresh ricotta and warm apple strudel—the owner's grandmother was making it in the kitchen. The plateau stays cool even on hot summer days because of the forest canopy; perfect for escaping Bolzano's heat. Go on a weekday morning for near-total solitude.

trails.access_info: From Bolzano city center, take the Renon cable car (Rittner Bahn) which departs every 10 minutes from Bolzano-Gries district. The cable car arrives at Renon (1,370m); park at the cable car base station (paid parking).

transport.car: Cable car base station parking: 150 spaces, €1.80/hour. Alternative: drive via the mountain road if you prefer (20 mins from city center).
transport.bus: SAD line 204 from Bolzano center to Renon cable car base (15 mins, hourly service year-round)

trailhead_info.parking: Use cable car base parking (150 spaces, €1.80/hour) or park in Bolzano and ride the cable car
trailhead_info.facilities: Café and WC at the upper cable car station

crowding.quiet_tip: Go on weekday mornings; this trail is less crowded than Dolomite peaks but busier than remote valleys

trails.weather_notes: The plateau is often cooler and breezier than Bolzano; bring a light jacket even in summer.
highlights: Stumbling upon a working farmstead where you can buy fresh, warm butter and milk directly from the farmer
The cool beech forest section in mid-afternoon when shafts of golden light pierce the canopy
Maria Saal church perched on its hilltop, surrounded by wildflower meadows and distant Dolomite views

trails.name: Val Sarentino Ibex & Waterfall Trail
trails.tagline: A wild valley traverse featuring Alpine ibex herds, cascading waterfalls, and remote mountain rifugios.
trails.description: This out-and-back trail ascends a remote valley from Sarentino village into high Alpine terrain where wildlife is more abundant than people, with regular Alpine ibex sightings in the upper sections and cascading waterfalls that tumble over moss-covered rocks throughout the route. The valley narrowed dramatically as elevation increases, creating intimate amphitheater walls of grey limestone that frame distant glacier remnants. The upper trail terminates at a small crystal-clear alpine lake beneath a rocky cirque, where marmots whistle warnings and the silence feels profound.

pois.name: Sarenta Waterfall
pois.description: A 40-meter cascade tumbling over moss-covered limestone, most dramatic in June-July during snowmelt.

pois.name: Alpine Ibex Viewpoint
pois.description: A rocky outlook at 2,400m elevation where herds of wild ibex with magnificent curved horns often graze on the surrounding slopes.

pois.name: Sarntal Rifugio
pois.description: A remote mountain hut serving hearty South Tyrolean meals and offering overnight accommodations for serious hikers.

pois.name: Sarentino Summit Alpine Lake
pois.description: A pristine glacial pool at trail's end, reflecting surrounding peaks and providing an unexpected wild swimming opportunity.

josephineNote.en: I saw 15 wild ibex on the rocky slopes above the Ibex Viewpoint in early July—they were relaxed and used to hikers at that distance. The Sarenta Waterfall thundered with snowmelt; if you go in late August it's just a trickle. Stay at Sarntal Rifugio overnight if you can; the sunset and star visibility are incredible, and the owner makes the best speck (cured meat) I've tasted in Tyrol.

trails.access_info: From Bolzano, drive southeast 30km on the SS44 toward Sarentino village. Park at the Sarentino village center (free parking); the trailhead starts at the yellow hiking sign near the village church, ascending along the Sarenta stream.

transport.car: Free parking in Sarentino village center (20 spaces); overflow parking available 100m away
transport.bus: SAD line 252 from Bolzano to Sarentino village (45 mins, limited service—check schedule as only 2-3 daily buses)

trailhead_info.parking: Free village parking: 20 spaces at center, additional overflow parking nearby
trailhead_info.facilities: Small village store and café at trailhead; main facilities at Sarntal Rifugio (2.5 hours up trail)

crowding.quiet_tip: This valley sees fewer hikers than famous Dolomite trails; even peak season offers solitude. Go on any day and you'll likely see fewer than 20 other people.

trails.weather_notes: Weather changes rapidly in this narrow valley; afternoon thunderstorms develop quickly in summer; start by 7am and plan to descend by 2pm.
highlights: Watching Alpine ibex herds grazing on near-vertical rocky slopes, with their massive curved horns silhouetted against the sky
The moment the waterfall comes into view—a thundering cascade of white water framed by moss-covered grey limestone
The final push to the alpine lake where the valley's rocky walls create an amphitheater and silence is near-total

trails.name: Merano Water Trail (Waalweg)
trails.tagline: A gentle centuries-old irrigation channel walk through orchards and vineyards with mountain backdrops.
trails.description: The Waalweg follows a historic water channel built in medieval times to irrigate the apple orchards and vineyards of the Merano valley, offering a peaceful, nearly flat walk with constant views of snow-capped peaks. The path meanders through tunnels carved into rock, past ancient apple trees, and alongside refreshing mountain streams, with occasional rifugios for refreshment. This is one of South Tyrol's most accessible high-altitude walks, suitable for all ages and fitness levels.

pois.name: Merano Town Center
pois.description: Historic spa town with pastel Art Nouveau architecture, famous Kurhaus gardens, and excellent restaurants.

pois.name: Castello Tirolo
pois.description: 13th-century castle perched above Merano offering regional history museum and panoramic terraces.

pois.name: Mountain Stream Crossings
pois.description: Multiple points where the Waalweg crosses sparkling alpine streams with Ortler massif views.

josephineNote.en: The Waalweg is absolutely magical in late May when apple blossoms still linger and the mountain streams are full from snowmelt—the sound is incredible. Start from Merano's Passer bridge (not the Avelengo trailhead) and you'll have the best light. Bring a small container; local apple farmers often leave fresh fruit by the trail in harvest season (September–October).

trails.access_info: From Merano town center, walk to the Passer River bridge (Passerbrücke) near the Kurhaus. The Waalweg trailhead is signed from here; it's a 15-minute walk from the town center.

transport.car: Merano town parking: multiple municipal lots, €1.50/hour; Kurhaus lot has 300 spaces
transport.bus: SAD line 260 from Bolzano to Merano (1 hour, hourly service); line 204 from Bolzano also stops in Merano

trailhead_info.parking: Multiple Merano town lots; Kurhaus lot 300 spaces, €1.50/hour
trailhead_info.facilities: Full facilities in Merano center; cafes at Passerbrücke

crowding.quiet_tip: Visit in April or October for solitude; early mornings any season offer fewer tourists.

trails.weather_notes: This valley can be surprisingly warm and sunny even in shoulder seasons; spring and fall are ideal to avoid summer crowds and heat.
highlights: Medieval water channel carved through living rock with views of the Ortler massif beyond apple orchards
Traditional Ladin stone tunnels where the Waalweg ducks through mountainsides, with cool mountain air and echoing water sounds
Merano's Belle Époque architecture and thermal gardens as a finishing point for lunch and refreshment

trails.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Circuit
trails.tagline: The Dolomites' most recognizable peaks demand respect on this exposed, technical alpine circumnavigation.
trails.description: This demanding loop circles the UNESCO-listed Tre Cime di Lavaredo—three monumental limestone pillars rising sheer from alpine meadows—crossing exposed ridges with chains and cables for protection, scrambling over technical rock sections, and offering unparalleled views into Dolomite geology. The trail traverses multiple high passes, visits hidden lakes cradled between peaks, and requires solid scrambling ability, good fitness, and mountain experience. Afternoon electrical storms are a real hazard on this exposed ridge traverse.

pois.name: Tre Cime Peaks
pois.description: Three iconic limestone towers (2,999m, 2,999m, 2,959m) creating the most photographed skyline in the Dolomites.

pois.name: Rifugio Auronzo
pois.description: Dramatic rifugio perched on exposed terrain below the main peaks, serving hearty mountain fare and offering emergency shelter.

pois.name: Sorau Lake
pois.description: Remote turquoise alpine lake nestled at 2,600m between steep scree slopes and offering the only water refill on the route.

pois.name: Via Ferrata Sections
pois.description: Protected scrambling passages with fixed cables offering thrilling exposure and intimate views of vertical rock faces.

josephineNote.en: I did this loop on a clear September morning starting at 6am and it was absolutely spine-tingling—the exposure on the ridges is real and humbling. The chain-protected sections are well-maintained but require confidence on steep rock. Most crucially: if you see any cloud building by noon, descend immediately. I've seen lightning strike Rifugio Auronzo from 20km away. Bring proper climbing shoes, not hiking boots.

trails.access_info: From Cortina d'Ampezzo, drive towards Tre Cime National Park via the SR48A (toll road, €10). The main trailhead is at Auronzo parking area (1,320m), 45 minutes drive from Cortina.

transport.car: Auronzo parking: 2,000 spaces, €5/day; arrive by 7am in July–August or risk full lots by 9am
transport.bus: No direct bus service; shuttle from Cortina d'Ampezzo town (10€, 30 mins, summer only) operates July–August daily

trailhead_info.parking: 2,000 spaces at Auronzo; €5/day; very full by 10am in peak season
trailhead_info.facilities: WC at parking; full facilities at Rifugio Auronzo (1.5 hours up trail)

crowding.quiet_tip: Start before 7am or visit mid-week in early September when Italian schools resume; the crowds thin dramatically.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon thunderstorms are very common June–August; the exposed ridges have zero shelter and attract frequent lightning strikes.
highlights: The dramatic exposed ridge traverse with fixed cable sections offering vertiginous views into Dolomite canyons
Close-range views of three monumental limestone towers that define the Dolomite skyline, accessible only from this loop
Remote Sorau Lake cupped between scree slopes at 2,600m, a hidden gem for brave souls to refill water

trails.name: Passo Gardena to Langkofel Ridge
trails.tagline: A classic alpine traverse linking the Dolomites' most dramatic ridgelines with stunning exposure and technical sections.
trails.description: This demanding ridge walk traverses from Passo Gardena across the Langkofel (Sassolungo) ridge, one of the Dolomites' most distinctive sawtooth mountain ranges, with constant exposure, scrambling, and via ferrata sections secured by chains. The ridge offers 360-degree views of the surrounding Dolomite UNESCO world heritage landscape and passes through a landscape of dramatic towers, hidden tarns, and wind-sculptured limestone formations. The point-to-point format allows for a cable car descent, avoiding dangerous scree fields.

pois.name: Langkofel Summit
pois.description: The iconic 3,181m sawtooth peak offering panoramic views across the entire Dolomite range and Italian Alps.

pois.name: Passo Gardena
pois.description: High pass at 2,121m between Val Gardena and Val di Fassa, with rifugios and cable car access.

pois.name: Via Ferrata Chains
pois.description: Multiple protected scrambling sections with fixed cables offering exhilarating ridge exposure.

josephineNote.en: The Langkofel ridge is absolutely magical at sunrise when you're above the clouds and the jagged towers cast long shadows—I started from Passo Gardena at 6:30am and had the entire ridge to myself. The via ferrata sections are straightforward if you're comfortable with exposure; just move deliberately. Skip this if you're afraid of heights. The cable car down from Rifugio Fedare is mandatory if you're tired; the scree descent is tedious and hard on knees.

trails.access_info: Drive to Passo Gardena (2,121m) via the SR163 from Ortisei or the SR242 from Canazei. The trailhead is at the pass, where multiple rifugios and cable cars provide services.

transport.car: Passo Gardena parking: 600 spaces, €2/day; very full weekends July–August
transport.bus: SAD line 350 from Bolzano to Ortisei (30 mins), then shuttle from Ortisei to Passo Gardena (20 mins, summer only)

trailhead_info.parking: 600 spaces at Passo Gardena; €2/day; arrive by 8am on weekends
trailhead_info.facilities: Multiple rifugios at pass with full WC and restaurant services

crowding.quiet_tip: Visit on weekdays in September for significantly fewer people; the ridge is much more peaceful mid-week.

trails.weather_notes: Afternoon cloud and occasional thunderstorms are common; the exposed ridge offers no shelter and attracts lightning frequently.
highlights: The sawtooth Langkofel ridge with dramatic exposure, via ferrata chains, and 360-degree Dolomite panoramas
The iconic profile of Sassolungo from multiple angles, one of the Dolomites' most recognizable mountain silhouettes
Hidden tarns and wind-carved limestone formations creating an otherworldly alpine landscape

trails.name: Guncina Forest Loop
trails.tagline: A gentle woodland ramble through beech forests directly above Bolzano with surprising mountain views.
trails.description: This accessible loop climbs gently through pristine beech and spruce forests from the Bolzano suburbs, emerging into alpine meadows with surprising views of the Dolomite peaks rising beyond the city. The forest path is shaded and cool even in summer, perfect for families and less experienced walkers, and the open meadow sections offer refreshing perspectives of the Ortler and Adamello ranges. Traditional stone rifugios dot the route, offering refreshment with a view.

pois.name: Rifugio Guncina
pois.description: Family-friendly mountain hut at 1,600m serving traditional South Tyrol dumplings and schnitzel with alpine views.

pois.name: Bolzano City View
pois.description: Panoramic meadows offering bird's-eye views of Bolzano's medieval center and surrounding wine country.

pois.name: Beech Forest Section
pois.description: Cool, shaded woodland trail with moss-covered rocks and natural spring water trickles.

josephineNote.en: The Guncina loop is perfect for a lazy Sunday morning—park at the bottom, do the easy forest loop, have lunch at Rifugio Guncina (order the spinach and ricotta dumplings), and be back in Bolzano by 3pm. The beech forest is absolutely glorious in autumn when the leaves turn golden; spring is lovely too with wildflowers in the meadows. Dogs love the forest floor; bring a portable water bowl.

trails.access_info: From central Bolzano, drive north on Via Guncina (follow signs for 'Guncina/Kohlern') for 3km to the parking area at 1,190m. The loop trailhead is clearly marked from the parking area.

transport.car: Guncina parking: 200 spaces, free; alternative street parking available
transport.bus: SAD line 12 from Bolzano town center to Guncina (20 mins, hourly service year-round) stops 500m from trailhead

trailhead_info.parking: 200 spaces, free; street parking available if full
trailhead_info.facilities: Cafe at parking area; full facilities at Rifugio Guncina (1 hour up trail)

crowding.quiet_tip: Visit weekday mornings year-round; even in summer the beech forest loop sees few hikers compared to alpine routes.

trails.weather_notes: The beech forest provides excellent shade even in hot summer; spring rain is common but rarely heavy enough to close the trail.
highlights: Cool, moss-covered beech forest with natural spring water and dappled sunlight, a respite from Bolzano's summer heat
Alpine meadows emerging from forest with sudden views of Bolzano nestled in wine country far below
Rifugio Guncina's terrace serving fresh regional dumplings with Dolomite views as a perfect lunch reward

trails.name: Tre Cime di Lavaredo Circuit
trails.tagline: Hike past three UNESCO World Heritage limestone spires in one of Europe's most dramatic mountain amphitheaters.
trails.description: This legendary circuit winds through the Sorau Valley and circles the iconic Tre Cime—three colossal limestone towers that dominate the Dolomites skyline. The trail passes Rifugio Auronzo and Rifugio Lavaredo, offering dramatic vertical climbs and descents with exposure that demands respect and focus. Every switchback reveals another jaw-dropping perspective of these 3,000m+ spires, UNESCO-recognized for their otherworldly geology.

pois.name: Grande Cima
pois.description: The largest of the three towers at 3,094m, its sheer north face dominates the vista.

pois.name: Rifugio Auronzo
pois.description: A mountain hut perched at 2,602m offering refuge and hearty Tyrolean cuisine.

pois.name: Sorau Valley
pois.description: A glacial valley beneath the Tre Cime offering sweeping panoramas of all three peaks.

pois.name: Rifugio Lavaredo
pois.description: Another atmospheric hut at 2,344m with legendary strudel and mountain hospitality.

josephineNote.en: The descent from Rifugio Auronzo toward the Sorau Valley involves steep switchbacks with cable-assisted sections—if you're not comfortable with exposure or via ferrata, turn back at the valley floor. The real magic isn't at the rifugios but in the Sorau Valley itself around midday when light hits the north faces—plan your timing accordingly.
